constructors.name: Vector3
constructors.summary: Constructs a default-initialized [Vector3] with all components set to [code]0[/code].

constructors.name: Vector3
constructors.summary: Constructs a [Vector3] as a copy of the given [Vector3].

constructors.name: Vector3
constructors.signature: public Vector3(float x, float y, float z)
constructors.summary: Returns a [Vector3] with the given components.

members.name: Abs
members.summary: Returns a new vector with all components in absolute values (i.e. positive).

members.name: AngleTo
members.summary: Returns the unsigned minimum angle to the given vector, in radians.

members.name: BezierDerivative
members.signature: public Vector3 BezierDerivative(Vector3 control1, Vector3 control2, Vector3 end, float t)
members.summary: Returns the derivative at the given [param t] on the [url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/B%C3%A9zier_curve]Bézier curve[/url] defined by this vector and the given [param control_1], [param control_2], and [param end] points.

members.name: BezierInterpolate
members.signature: public Vector3 BezierInterpolate(Vector3 control1, Vector3 control2, Vector3 end, float t)
members.summary: Returns the point at the given [param t] on the [url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/B%C3%A9zier_curve]Bézier curve[/url] defined by this vector and the given [param control_1], [param control_2], and [param end] points.

members.name: Bounce
members.summary: Returns the vector "bounced off" from a plane defined by the given normal [param n]. [b]Note:[/b] [method bounce] performs the operation that most engines and frameworks call [code skip-lint]reflect()[/code].

members.name: Ceil
members.summary: Returns a new vector with all components rounded up (towards positive infinity).

members.name: Clamp
members.signature: public Vector3 Clamp(Vector3 min, Vector3 max)
members.summary: Returns a new vector with all components clamped between the components of [param min] and [param max], by running [method @GlobalScope.clamp] on each component.

members.name: Clampf
members.signature: public Vector3 Clampf(float min, float max)
members.summary: Returns a new vector with all components clamped between [param min] and [param max], by running [method @GlobalScope.clamp] on each component.

members.name: Cross
members.summary: Returns the cross product of this vector and [param with]. This returns a vector perpendicular to both this and [param with], which would be the normal vector of the plane defined by the two vectors. As there are two such vectors, in opposite directions, this method returns the vector defined by a right-handed coordinate system. If the two vectors are parallel this returns an empty vector, making it useful for testing if two vectors are parallel.

members.name: CubicInterpolate
members.signature: public Vector3 CubicInterpolate(Vector3 b, Vector3 preA, Vector3 postB, float weight)
members.summary: Performs a cubic interpolation between this vector and [param b] using [param pre_a] and [param post_b] as handles, and returns the result at position [param weight]. [param weight] is on the range of 0.0 to 1.0, representing the amount of interpolation.

members.name: CubicInterpolateInTime
members.signature: public Vector3 CubicInterpolateInTime(Vector3 b, Vector3 preA, Vector3 postB, float weight, float bT, float preAT, float postBT)
members.summary: Performs a cubic interpolation between this vector and [param b] using [param pre_a] and [param post_b] as handles, and returns the result at position [param weight]. [param weight] is on the range of 0.0 to 1.0, representing the amount of interpolation. It can perform smoother interpolation than [method cubic_interpolate] by the time values.

members.name: DirectionTo
members.summary: Returns the normalized vector pointing from this vector to [param to]. This is equivalent to using [code](b - a).normalized()[/code].

members.name: DistanceSquaredTo
members.summary: Returns the squared [url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Euclidean_distance]Euclidean distance[/url] between this vector and [param to]. This method runs faster than [method distance_to], so prefer it if you need to compare vectors or need the squared distance for some formula.

members.name: DistanceTo
members.summary: Returns the [url=https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Euclidean_distance]Euclidean distance[/url] between this vector and [param to].

members.name: Dot
members.summary: Returns the dot product of this vector and [param with]. This can be used to compare the angle between two vectors. For example, this can be used to determine whether an enemy is facing the player. The dot product will be [code]0[/code] for a right angle (90 degrees), greater than 0 for angles narrower than 90 degrees and lower than 0 for angles wider than 90 degrees. When using unit (normalized) vectors, the result will always be between [code]-1.0[/code] (180 degree angle) when the vectors are facing opposite directions, and [code]1.0[/code] (0 degree angle) when the vectors are aligned. [b]Note:[/b] [code]a.dot(b)[/code] is equivalent to [code]b.dot(a)[/code].

members.name: Floor
members.summary: Returns a new vector with all components rounded down (towards negative infinity).

members.name: Inverse
members.summary: Returns the inverse of the vector. This is the same as [code]Vector3(1.0 / v.x, 1.0 / v.y, 1.0 / v.z)[/code].

members.name: IsEqualApprox
members.summary: Returns [code]true[/code] if this vector and [param to] are approximately equal, by running [method @GlobalScope.is_equal_approx] on each component.

members.name: IsFinite
members.summary: Returns [code]true[/code] if this vector is finite, by calling [method @GlobalScope.is_finite] on each component.

members.name: IsNormalized
members.summary: Returns [code]true[/code] if the vector is normalized, i.e. its length is approximately equal to 1.

members.name: IsZeroApprox
members.summary: Returns [code]true[/code] if this vector's values are approximately zero, by running [method @GlobalScope.is_zero_approx] on each component. This method is faster than using [method is_equal_approx] with one value as a zero vector.

members.name: Length
members.summary: Returns the length (magnitude) of this vector.

members.name: LengthSquared
members.summary: Returns the squared length (squared magnitude) of this vector. This method runs faster than [method length], so prefer it if you need to compare vectors or need the squared distance for some formula.

members.name: Lerp
members.signature: public Vector3 Lerp(Vector3 to, float weight)
members.summary: Returns the result of the linear interpolation between this vector and [param to] by amount [param weight]. [param weight] is on the range of [code]0.0[/code] to [code]1.0[/code], representing the amount of interpolation.

members.name: LimitLength
members.summary: Returns the vector with a maximum length by limiting its length to [param length]. If the vector is non-finite, the result is undefined.

members.name: Max
members.summary: Returns the component-wise maximum of this and [param with], equivalent to [code]Vector3(maxf(x, with.x), maxf(y, with.y), maxf(z, with.z))[/code].

members.name: MaxAxisIndex
members.summary: Returns the axis of the vector's highest value. See [code]AXIS_*[/code] constants. If all components are equal, this method returns [constant AXIS_X].

members.name: Maxf
members.summary: Returns the component-wise maximum of this and [param with], equivalent to [code]Vector3(maxf(x, with), maxf(y, with), maxf(z, with))[/code].

members.name: Min
members.summary: Returns the component-wise minimum of this and [param with], equivalent to [code]Vector3(minf(x, with.x), minf(y, with.y), minf(z, with.z))[/code].

members.name: MinAxisIndex
members.summary: Returns the axis of the vector's lowest value. See [code]AXIS_*[/code] constants. If all components are equal, this method returns [constant AXIS_Z].

members.name: Minf
members.summary: Returns the component-wise minimum of this and [param with], equivalent to [code]Vector3(minf(x, with), minf(y, with), minf(z, with))[/code].

members.name: MoveToward
members.signature: public Vector3 MoveToward(Vector3 to, float delta)
members.summary: Returns a new vector moved toward [param to] by the fixed [param delta] amount. Will not go past the final value.

members.name: Normalized
members.summary: Returns the result of scaling the vector to unit length. Equivalent to [code]v / v.length()[/code]. Returns [code](0, 0, 0)[/code] if [code]v.length() == 0[/code]. See also [method is_normalized]. [b]Note:[/b] This function may return incorrect values if the input vector length is near zero.

members.name: OctahedronDecode
members.summary: Returns the [Vector3] from an octahedral-compressed form created using [method octahedron_encode] (stored as a [Vector2]).

members.name: OctahedronEncode
members.summary: Returns the octahedral-encoded (oct32) form of this [Vector3] as a [Vector2]. Since a [Vector2] occupies 1/3 less memory compared to [Vector3], this form of compression can be used to pass greater amounts of [method normalized] [Vector3]s without increasing storage or memory requirements. See also [method octahedron_decode]. [b]Note:[/b] [method octahedron_encode] can only be used for [method normalized] vectors. [method octahedron_encode] does [i]not[/i] check whether this [Vector3] is normalized, and will return a value that does not decompress to the original value if the [Vector3] is not normalized. [b]Note:[/b] Octahedral compression is [i]lossy[/i], although visual differences are rarely perceptible in real world scenarios.

members.name: Outer
members.summary: Returns the outer product with [param with].

members.name: Posmod
members.summary: Returns a vector composed of the [method @GlobalScope.fposmod] of this vector's components and [param mod].

members.name: Posmodv
members.summary: Returns a vector composed of the [method @GlobalScope.fposmod] of this vector's components and [param modv]'s components.

members.name: Project
members.summary: Returns a new vector resulting from projecting this vector onto the given vector [param b]. The resulting new vector is parallel to [param b]. See also [method slide]. [b]Note:[/b] If the vector [param b] is a zero vector, the components of the resulting new vector will be [constant @GDScript.NAN].

members.name: Reflect
members.summary: Returns the result of reflecting the vector through a plane defined by the given normal vector [param n]. [b]Note:[/b] [method reflect] differs from what other engines and frameworks call [code skip-lint]reflect()[/code]. In other engines, [code skip-lint]reflect()[/code] returns the result of the vector reflected by the given plane. The reflection thus passes through the given normal. While in Godot the reflection passes through the plane and can be thought of as bouncing off the normal. See also [method bounce] which does what most engines call [code skip-lint]reflect()[/code].

members.name: Rotated
members.signature: public Vector3 Rotated(Vector3 axis, float angle)
members.summary: Returns the result of rotating this vector around a given axis by [param angle] (in radians). The axis must be a normalized vector. See also [method @GlobalScope.deg_to_rad].

members.name: Round
members.summary: Returns a new vector with all components rounded to the nearest integer, with halfway cases rounded away from zero.

members.name: Sign
members.summary: Returns a new vector with each component set to [code]1.0[/code] if it's positive, [code]-1.0[/code] if it's negative, and [code]0.0[/code] if it's zero. The result is identical to calling [method @GlobalScope.sign] on each component.

members.name: SignedAngleTo
members.signature: public float SignedAngleTo(Vector3 to, Vector3 axis)
members.summary: Returns the signed angle to the given vector, in radians. The sign of the angle is positive in a counter-clockwise direction and negative in a clockwise direction when viewed from the side specified by the [param axis].

members.name: Slerp
members.signature: public Vector3 Slerp(Vector3 to, float weight)
members.summary: Returns the result of spherical linear interpolation between this vector and [param to], by amount [param weight]. [param weight] is on the range of 0.0 to 1.0, representing the amount of interpolation. This method also handles interpolating the lengths if the input vectors have different lengths. For the special case of one or both input vectors having zero length, this method behaves like [method lerp].

members.name: Slide
members.summary: Returns a new vector resulting from sliding this vector along a plane with normal [param n]. The resulting new vector is perpendicular to [param n], and is equivalent to this vector minus its projection on [param n]. See also [method project]. [b]Note:[/b] The vector [param n] must be normalized. See also [method normalized].

members.name: Snapped
members.summary: Returns a new vector with each component snapped to the nearest multiple of the corresponding component in [param step]. This can also be used to round the components to an arbitrary number of decimals.

members.name: Snappedf
members.summary: Returns a new vector with each component snapped to the nearest multiple of [param step]. This can also be used to round the components to an arbitrary number of decimals.

members.name: X
members.summary: The vector's X component. Also accessible by using the index position [code][0][/code].

members.name: Y
members.summary: The vector's Y component. Also accessible by using the index position [code][1][/code].

members.name: Z
members.summary: The vector's Z component. Also accessible by using the index position [code][2][/code].

values.name: AXIS_X
values.summary: Enumerated value for the X axis. Returned by [method max_axis_index] and [method min_axis_index].

values.name: AXIS_Y
values.summary: Enumerated value for the Y axis. Returned by [method max_axis_index] and [method min_axis_index].

values.name: AXIS_Z
values.summary: Enumerated value for the Z axis. Returned by [method max_axis_index] and [method min_axis_index].

constants.name: AXIS_X
constants.summary: Enumerated value for the X axis. Returned by [method max_axis_index] and [method min_axis_index].

constants.name: AXIS_Y
constants.summary: Enumerated value for the Y axis. Returned by [method max_axis_index] and [method min_axis_index].

constants.name: AXIS_Z
constants.summary: Enumerated value for the Z axis. Returned by [method max_axis_index] and [method min_axis_index].

constants.name: ZERO
constants.summary: Zero vector, a vector with all components set to [code]0[/code].

constants.name: ONE
constants.summary: One vector, a vector with all components set to [code]1[/code].

constants.name: INF
constants.summary: Infinity vector, a vector with all components set to [constant @GDScript.INF].

constants.name: LEFT
constants.summary: Left unit vector. Represents the local direction of left, and the global direction of west.

constants.name: RIGHT
constants.summary: Right unit vector. Represents the local direction of right, and the global direction of east.

constants.name: FORWARD
constants.summary: Forward unit vector. Represents the local direction of forward, and the global direction of north. Keep in mind that the forward direction for lights, cameras, etc is different from 3D assets like characters, which face towards the camera by convention. Use [constant Vector3.MODEL_FRONT] and similar constants when working in 3D asset space.

constants.name: BACK
constants.summary: Back unit vector. Represents the local direction of back, and the global direction of south.

constants.name: MODEL_LEFT
constants.summary: Unit vector pointing towards the left side of imported 3D assets.

constants.name: MODEL_RIGHT
constants.summary: Unit vector pointing towards the right side of imported 3D assets.

constants.name: MODEL_TOP
constants.summary: Unit vector pointing towards the top side (up) of imported 3D assets.

constants.name: MODEL_BOTTOM
constants.summary: Unit vector pointing towards the bottom side (down) of imported 3D assets.

constants.name: MODEL_FRONT
constants.summary: Unit vector pointing towards the front side (facing forward) of imported 3D assets.

constants.name: MODEL_REAR
constants.summary: Unit vector pointing towards the rear side (back) of imported 3D assets.

operators.name: operator !=
operators.summary: Returns [code]true[/code] if the vectors are not equal. [b]Note:[/b] Due to floating-point precision errors, consider using [method is_equal_approx] instead, which is more reliable. [b]Note:[/b] Vectors with [constant @GDScript.NAN] elements don't behave the same as other vectors. Therefore, the results from this operator may not be accurate if NaNs are included.

operators.name: operator *
operators.summary: Inversely transforms (multiplies) the [Vector3] by the given [Basis] matrix, under the assumption that the basis is orthonormal (i.e. rotation/reflection is fine, scaling/skew is not). [code]vector * basis[/code] is equivalent to [code]basis.transposed() * vector[/code]. See [method Basis.transposed]. For transforming by inverse of a non-orthonormal basis (e.g. with scaling) [code]basis.inverse() * vector[/code] can be used instead. See [method Basis.inverse].

operators.name: operator *
operators.summary: Inversely transforms (multiplies) the [Vector3] by the given [Quaternion]. [code]vector * quaternion[/code] is equivalent to [code]quaternion.inverse() * vector[/code]. See [method Quaternion.inverse].

operators.name: operator *
operators.summary: Inversely transforms (multiplies) the [Vector3] by the given [Transform3D] transformation matrix, under the assumption that the transformation basis is orthonormal (i.e. rotation/reflection is fine, scaling/skew is not). [code]vector * transform[/code] is equivalent to [code]transform.inverse() * vector[/code]. See [method Transform3D.inverse]. For transforming by inverse of an affine transformation (e.g. with scaling) [code]transform.affine_inverse() * vector[/code] can be used instead. See [method Transform3D.affine_inverse].

operators.name: operator *
operators.summary: Multiplies each component of the [Vector3] by the components of the given [Vector3]. [codeblock] print(Vector3(10, 20, 30) * Vector3(3, 4, 5)) # Prints (30.0, 80.0, 150.0) [/codeblock]

operators.name: operator *
operators.summary: Multiplies each component of the [Vector3] by the given [float].

operators.name: operator *
operators.summary: Multiplies each component of the [Vector3] by the given [int].

operators.name: operator +
operators.summary: Adds each component of the [Vector3] by the components of the given [Vector3]. [codeblock] print(Vector3(10, 20, 30) + Vector3(3, 4, 5)) # Prints (13.0, 24.0, 35.0) [/codeblock]

operators.name: operator -
operators.summary: Subtracts each component of the [Vector3] by the components of the given [Vector3]. [codeblock] print(Vector3(10, 20, 30) - Vector3(3, 4, 5)) # Prints (7.0, 16.0, 25.0) [/codeblock]

operators.name: operator /
operators.summary: Divides each component of the [Vector3] by the components of the given [Vector3]. [codeblock] print(Vector3(10, 20, 30) / Vector3(2, 5, 3)) # Prints (5.0, 4.0, 10.0) [/codeblock]

operators.name: operator /
operators.summary: Divides each component of the [Vector3] by the given [float].

operators.name: operator /
operators.summary: Divides each component of the [Vector3] by the given [int].

operators.name: operator <
operators.summary: Compares two [Vector3] vectors by first checking if the X value of the left vector is less than the X value of the [param right] vector. If the X values are exactly equal, then it repeats this check with the Y values of the two vectors, and then with the Z values. This operator is useful for sorting vectors. [b]Note:[/b] Vectors with [constant @GDScript.NAN] elements don't behave the same as other vectors. Therefore, the results from this operator may not be accurate if NaNs are included.

operators.name: operator <=
operators.summary: Compares two [Vector3] vectors by first checking if the X value of the left vector is less than or equal to the X value of the [param right] vector. If the X values are exactly equal, then it repeats this check with the Y values of the two vectors, and then with the Z values. This operator is useful for sorting vectors. [b]Note:[/b] Vectors with [constant @GDScript.NAN] elements don't behave the same as other vectors. Therefore, the results from this operator may not be accurate if NaNs are included.

operators.name: operator ==
operators.summary: Returns [code]true[/code] if the vectors are exactly equal. [b]Note:[/b] Due to floating-point precision errors, consider using [method is_equal_approx] instead, which is more reliable. [b]Note:[/b] Vectors with [constant @GDScript.NAN] elements don't behave the same as other vectors. Therefore, the results from this operator may not be accurate if NaNs are included.

operators.name: operator >
operators.summary: Compares two [Vector3] vectors by first checking if the X value of the left vector is greater than the X value of the [param right] vector. If the X values are exactly equal, then it repeats this check with the Y values of the two vectors, and then with the Z values. This operator is useful for sorting vectors. [b]Note:[/b] Vectors with [constant @GDScript.NAN] elements don't behave the same as other vectors. Therefore, the results from this operator may not be accurate if NaNs are included.

operators.name: operator >=
operators.summary: Compares two [Vector3] vectors by first checking if the X value of the left vector is greater than or equal to the X value of the [param right] vector. If the X values are exactly equal, then it repeats this check with the Y values of the two vectors, and then with the Z values. This operator is useful for sorting vectors. [b]Note:[/b] Vectors with [constant @GDScript.NAN] elements don't behave the same as other vectors. Therefore, the results from this operator may not be accurate if NaNs are included.

operators.name: operator []
operators.summary: Access vector components using their [param index]. [code]v[0][/code] is equivalent to [code]v.x[/code], [code]v[1][/code] is equivalent to [code]v.y[/code], and [code]v[2][/code] is equivalent to [code]v.z[/code].

operators.name: operator unary+
operators.summary: Returns the same value as if the [code]+[/code] was not there. Unary [code]+[/code] does nothing, but sometimes it can make your code more readable.

operators.name: operator unary-
operators.summary: Returns the negative value of the [Vector3]. This is the same as writing [code]Vector3(-v.x, -v.y, -v.z)[/code]. This operation flips the direction of the vector while keeping the same magnitude. With floats, the number zero can be either positive or negative.
